title: GetResponse vs Substack (2025): Which Is Better?
meta_description: Compare GetResponse and Substack. See pricing, features, and which email marketing tool suits your needs best.
intro: GetResponse and Substack are both email marketing platforms, but they serve different audiences and use different business models. GetResponse is a comprehensive marketing suite ideal for businesses running courses and webinars, while Substack is designed for individual writers and creators monetizing through paid subscriptions. Understanding their key differences will help you choose the right tool for your specific goals.

comparison_table.tool_b: Revenue-share (100% free, takes 10% of paid subscriptions)

comparison_table.tool_a: Email, webinars, landing pages, automation, courses, funnels
comparison_table.tool_b: Paid newsletters, podcasts, notes feed, recommendations

comparison_table.tool_a: Online course creators, webinar hosts, marketing teams
comparison_table.tool_b: Writers, journalists, independent creators with subscriber bases

choose_a_if: You need comprehensive marketing tools beyond email, including webinars, landing pages, and automation
You're building online courses or conversion funnels and want everything in one platform
You need an affiliate program to promote your platform and earn recurring commissions
choose_b_if: You're a writer or creator focused on building and monetizing a direct subscriber relationship
You want zero upfront costs with no paid tier requirements to start earning
You prefer simplicity and want to avoid learning complex marketing automation features

faq.a: It depends on your use case. GetResponse is better for businesses needing comprehensive marketing tools like webinars, courses, and automation. Substack excels for individual creators monetizing through direct paid subscriptions. GetResponse has higher ratings on review sites (G2: 4.2 vs 4.0), suggesting broader business appeal.

faq.q: Which is cheaper, GetResponse or Substack?
faq.a: Substack is free forever with no subscriber limits, making it cheaper for startups. However, Substack takes 10% of revenue from paid subscriptions, while GetResponse charges fixed monthly fees. For large subscriber bases generating significant revenue, GetResponse's flat fees may be more economical.

faq.q: Can I switch from Substack to GetResponse?
faq.a: Yes, most email platforms allow you to export your subscriber list from Substack and import it into GetResponse. However, you'll lose Substack's built-in audience discovery features and monetization infrastructure. Both platforms support CSV exports, making migration straightforward.
